content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (one of the descriptors in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List them with `ls .claude/tools/` and run `<path> --help` to learn each tool's purpose and usage. Invoke them via the Bash tool. Project MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful when you need a second opinion or a paper-style proof sketch the Lean side can then formalize.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (see "Subagents" below). One wrapper handles every subagent.
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`. Each starts with YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`) followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude session reads.
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents yourself.** The plan and review prompts have an auto-generated **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation that lists every enabled descriptor with its description, write-domain hint, and any `[MANDATORY]` flags. When you decide to invoke a specific subagent, read `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` for its full prompt and directive shape.
31	
32	**Invoke any subagent with the generic wrapper** (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, and writes the subagent's report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. It shells out to `archon subagent <name>`, so executions stream through the Archon JSONL log like a phase agent. The dispatch semaphore bounds total concurrent subagent processes by `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` automatically so deeper-nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Always dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). The wrapper returns when the subagent has finished and the report is on disk; only then write your session summary. Background dispatch leaves the parent's session permanently summarized as "running in background", which is wrong as soon as the subagent completes.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All state files are in `.archon/`:
51	
52	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Subagents | Review Agent | User |
53	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
54	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
55	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
56	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
57	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
58	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
59	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (`<name>-<slug>.md`) | read only | read |
60	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
61	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log moved to iter/iter-NNN/review.md) | read |
62	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | do not access | do not access | read (last K iters as context) | read |
63	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (last K iters as context) | do not access | do not access | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write (per-attempt detail) | do not access | do not access | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read** (write-capable subagents may rename file path only) | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | per descriptor: write-capable subagents (e.g. refactor) may edit per their declared write-domain (protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed); read-only subagents do not edit | do not edit | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
68	| `Challenges/<Name>.lean` | do not edit | fill sorries (when assigned) | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not edit | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | do not access | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not access | read |
70	
71	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow, run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. The plan agent invokes the refactor subagent (when a `refactor` descriptor is installed) via the generic `archon-subagent.py --name refactor` wrapper with a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/`. This keeps each loop-driven refactor independently logged and avoids any chance of stale-directive reuse. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, etc.) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise and prune them out as you rewrite.
72	
73	## Protected Declarations
74	
75	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
76	
77	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
78	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
79	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed.
80	
81	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
82	
83	The blueprint uses two active markers.
84	
85	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
86	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
87	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
88	
89	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between the prover and review phases each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves — let the script do it.
90	
91	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations remain the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
92	
93	## User Interaction
94	
95	Users provide hints in two places:
96	
97	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
98	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
99	
100	## Agent Roles
101	
102	### Plan Agent
103	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
104	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
105	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
106	- Optionally invoke **subagents** (discoverable via `ls .archon/subagents/`) by running `.claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> ...` via Bash, **before** writing prover objectives. See `.archon/prompts/plan.md` § "Subagent delegation" for the canonical ordering and full invocation pattern.
107	- Archive any subagent report you receive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it.
108	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
109	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
110	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
111	
112	### Prover Agent
113	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
114	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
115	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
116	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
117	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
118	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
119	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
120	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
121	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
122	
123	### Subagents
124	
125	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime — there is no fixed roster baked into this file. Each lives at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter + prompt body). To see what's available **this iteration**, run `ls .archon/subagents/` and read each descriptor's frontmatter for purpose, write-domain hint, and directive shape.
126	
127	Subagents are **not** part of the autonomous loop's standing phases. Phase agents (plan, review) invoke them on demand via the generic wrapper:
128	
129	```
130	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> --slug <slug> --directive-file <path> --write-domain '<glob>'
131	```
132	
133	Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. The dispatching agent reads the report when the wrapper returns and may then update `STRATEGY.md` / `PROGRESS.md` based on the findings.
134	
135	Some descriptors set `can_spawn: true`, meaning that subagent may itself dispatch children; the per-iter dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel` so deep trees stay bounded.
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
139	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
140	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
141	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
142	- Maintain the **semantic** blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE: ...` annotations, stale `\notready` removal — in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch `\leanok`; it's handled by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase that ran before you.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify PROGRESS.md
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	Two non-agent steps run automatically each iteration to reduce the burden on the agents above:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init**: at iter start, `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` is created so the plan + review agents have a stable destination for their per-iter narrative (`plan.md`, `review.md`, optional `objectives.md`). Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded across iters because per-iter content lives in the sidecars.
150	- **`sync_leanok`**: runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status. Replaces what used to be a multi-page review-agent task.
151	
152	`sync_leanok` writes an inner-git commit (`archon[NNN/marker-sync]`) so its output is auditable and revertable.

1	# Review Agent — Post-Session Proof Journal + Analysis
2	
3	You are the review agent. Your job is to: (1) analyze the most recent prover session with fine-grained detail, (2) produce a structured proof journal, (3) update project status, (4) maintain the blueprint markers that require *semantic* judgement (`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations), and (5) write recommendations for the next plan iteration.
4	
5	`\leanok` placement is handled deterministically by Archon's `sync_leanok` phase, which runs immediately before you. **Do not add or remove `\leanok` yourself.** If a `\leanok` you expect is missing, the underlying Lean source still has a sorry or doesn't compile — investigate, don't paper over.
6	
7	**Do NOT modify any .lean files. Do NOT write proofs.** You may run `lean_diagnostic_messages` / `lake env lean <file>` to verify compilation and sorry counts as part of your analysis. Otherwise you only read logs, analyze, and write journal/status/blueprint-marker files.
8	
9	## Iteration number — canonical
10	
11	Your invocation prompt contains `Archon iteration: NNN` and `Session number: M`. As of 2026 these are the **same number** — `session_M/` is always the review of `iter-NNN` (i.e. M == NNN). Use the iteration form (`iter-NNN`) when referencing the iteration in prose (`summary.md` metadata, recommendations titles like "Recommendations for the next plan-agent iteration (iter-{NNN+1:03d})") and the bare integer (`session_M`) when referring to the review-output directory. If older `summary.md` / `recommendations.md` files use the legacy independent session counter, treat them as drift from earlier agents — your new file uses the iteration number.
12	
13	## Step 1: Identify Context
14	
15	1. Check `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/` — count existing session folders to determine the current session number.
16	2. Run `find ${PROJECT_PATH} -name '*.lean' -not -path '*/.lake/*' -not -path '*/lake-packages/*' | xargs grep -c 'sorry' 2>/dev/null | grep -v ':0$' | awk -F: '{s+=$2} END {print s}'` for the current sorry count.
17	3. Run `git diff HEAD~1 --stat` to see what changed.
18	
19	## Step 2: Read Pre-processed Attempt Data (MANDATORY)
20	
21	**READ `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` COMPLETELY.** If this file does not exist or is empty, report it and proceed with what you can gather from task_results.
22	
23	The file contains:
24	
25	- Line 1: Summary stats (`type: "summary"` — total edits, goal checks, errors, files edited)
26	- Remaining lines: One event per tool call — edits, goal states, errors, lemma searches, builds
27	
28	**For each `code_change` event**: Record the actual code that was tried (old_text → new_text).
29	**For each `goal_state` event**: Record the Lean goal at that point.
30	**For each `diagnostics` event**: Record the Lean errors.
31	**For each `build` event**: Record whether it succeeded or failed.
32	
33	This is your PRIMARY data source. Task result files are supplementary.
34	
35	## Step 3: Read Recent History
36	
37	If previous session folders exist in `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/`, read `summary.md` from the **last 2 sessions**. Also read `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` if it exists.
38	
39	## Step 4: Write Proof Journal
40	
41	Create the session folder and write two files:
42	
43	```bash
44	mkdir -p .archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>
45	```
46	
47	### File A: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/summary.md`
48	
49	Must include:
50	- Session metadata (number, sorry count before/after, targets attempted)
51	- For EACH target attempted:
52	  - **Every significant attempt** with: tactic/code tried, Lean error received, goal state at that point
53	  - What was learned from each failed attempt
54	  - For solved targets: the final proof structure with key lemmas
55	- Key findings / proof patterns discovered
56	- Recommendations for next session
57	
58	### File B: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/milestones.jsonl`
59	
60	Each line MUST follow this JSON format — one entry per target theorem:
61	
62	```json
63	{
64	  "timestamp": "ISO-8601",
65	  "status": "solved|partial|blocked|not_started",
66	  "target": {
67	    "file": "path/to/File.lean",
68	    "theorem": "theorem_name"
69	  },
70	  "session": {
71	    "id": "session_N",
72	    "model": "model-name"
73	  },
74	  "findings": {
75	    "blocker": "description if blocked",
76	    "key_lemmas_used": ["lemma1", "lemma2"]
77	  },
78	  "attempts": [
79	    {
80	      "attempt": 1,
81	      "strategy": "what was tried",
82	      "code_tried": "actual Lean code or tactic",
83	      "line_number": "line number in the initial file where this code was attempted",
84	      "lean_error": "actual error message if failed",
85	      "goal_before": "the goal state before this attempt",
86	      "goal_after": "the goal state after this attempt",
87	      "result": "success|failed|partial",
88	      "insight": "what was learned from this attempt"
89	    }
90	  ],
91	  "next_steps": "..."
92	}
93	```
94	
95	**CRITICAL**: The `attempts` array must reflect ACTUAL attempts from the pre-processed data:
96	- If `attempts_raw.jsonl` shows 5 edits to a file, there should be multiple attempts recorded
97	- Each attempt must include `code_tried` (from edit events) and `lean_error` (from diagnostic events)
98	- Do NOT summarize multiple attempts as "tried various approaches" — list each one
99	
100	### File C: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/recommendations.md`
101	
102	Write concrete recommendations for the next plan agent iteration:
103	- Which targets are closest to completion and should be prioritized
104	- Which approaches showed promise but need more work
105	- Which targets are blocked and why (the plan agent should NOT assign these)
106	- Any reusable proof patterns discovered
107	
108	If your analysis shows the prover has hit the exact same blocker for several consecutive iterations on the same target, you should explicitly instruct the Plan Agent to avoid retrying the same approach without putting more effort into understanding the underlying issue.
109	
110	## Step 5: Update PROJECT_STATUS.md (Knowledge Base only)
111	
112	The per-session narrative (Overall Progress: total sorry, branches closed, solved/partial/blocked/untouched, this session's analysis) goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` — see the next subsection. PROJECT_STATUS.md carries only the cumulative Knowledge Base:
113	
114	```markdown
115	# Project Status
116	
117	## Knowledge Base
118	### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)
119	- <pattern name>: <description + key lemmas>
120	
121	### Known Blockers (do not retry)
122	- <target>: <reason>
123	
124	## Last Updated
125	<ISO timestamp>
126	```
127	
128	If an existing PROJECT_STATUS.md still carries an "Overall Progress" section from a prior project that grew it, leave the old content where it is (do not delete history) but stop appending to it. New session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`.
129	
130	### Per-iteration sidecars (split write)
131	
132	Your invocation prompt contains a `## Per-iteration sidecars` block that names `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` as the destination for THIS session's narrative. Split your writes accordingly:
133	
134	- **The per-session "Overall Progress" narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`**, NOT to PROJECT_STATUS.md's "Overall Progress" section. PROJECT_STATUS.md no longer carries a session log.
135	- **You DO keep updating PROJECT_STATUS.md's "Knowledge Base" section.** Cumulative non-obvious facts (errors not to reproduce, reusable proof patterns, Mathlib idioms that worked) still belong in the Knowledge Base. The Knowledge Base is the only growing-but-curated part of PROJECT_STATUS.md.
136	- **Format for `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`**: same fields as the Overall Progress block above (Total sorry, branches closed, solved/partial/blocked/untouched, plus any narrative analysis). Born-bounded — this file contains THIS session only, never a multi-session log.
137	- Older `iter/iter-MMM/review.md` files are on disk for you to read on demand, but the recent window is already injected in your prompt.
138	
139	## Step 6: Blueprint Markers
140	
141	`\leanok` placement is now handled deterministically by Archon's `sync_leanok` phase, which runs between the prover and you. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration in the Lean source, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. **Do not touch `\leanok` markers yourself.** If you see one missing where you expect it, the underlying file probably doesn't compile or still has a sorry — do not paper over that with a manual edit.
142	
143	Your remaining marker responsibilities are the ones that require semantic judgement:
144	
145	- **`\mathlibok`** (statement-block only) — declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias / direct reference). The deterministic script never adds or removes this; you decide based on the prover's task result.
146	- **`\lean{...}` macro maintenance** — if a prover renamed a declaration or chose a different name from the plan agent's hint, their task result will mention it. Update the `\lean{...}` line in the chapter to the correct Lean name. If a declaration was moved to a different file by a refactor, update the chapter's location references as needed.
147	- **`% NOTE: <reason>` annotations** — when a block is unformalized because the informal statement did not translate cleanly, add a `% NOTE: ...` comment explaining the obstacle so the plan agent sees it.
148	- **Stripping `\notready`** — if a `\notready` marker still sits on a block that the prover has now landed, remove it. The deterministic script does not manage `\notready`.
149	
150	### `\mathlibok` rules (your domain)
151	
152	Add `\mathlibok` inside the statement block when:
153	- The Lean side references a Mathlib name directly (`def foo := Mathlib.bar`, `theorem foo := Mathlib.bar`, or a simple `export Mathlib.Foo (bar)`), AND
154	- The Archon-side declaration itself contains no `sorry` and introduces no new proof obligation.
155	
156	If you add `\mathlibok`, no `\leanok` is needed on the proof block — the deterministic script will leave the proof block alone if there's no Lean proof body to verify.
157	
158	### Record what you changed
159	
160	In `session_<N>/summary.md`, include a "Blueprint markers updated" section listing **only the changes you personally made** (the deterministic script's `\leanok` adds/removes are committed separately as `archon[NNN/marker-sync]` and don't need to appear here):
161	
162	```markdown
163	## Blueprint markers updated (manual)
164	- `Algebra_WLocal.tex`, `lem:finite_closed`: added `\mathlibok` (backed by `Set.Finite.isClosed`)
165	- `Core.tex`, `thm:stacks_0A31`: added `% NOTE: prover reported translation gap, see task_results/Core.md`
166	- `Core.tex`, `thm:old_name`: stripped stale `\notready`
167	- `Core.tex`, `thm:foo`: corrected `\lean{Old.foo}` → `\lean{New.foo}` after refactor rename
168	```
169	
170	### File D: `.archon/TO_USER.md`
171	
172	While the agents should be autonomous, you might want to inform the user of any critical issues that require their attention. The content of `TO_USER.md` will be surfaced in the UI as an alert banner, however the user might not see it immediately or never see it at all (its content will be refreshed every iteration before the review agent is called). It might include for instance issues with the environment, critical missing dependencies, required user actions, etc. 
173	
174	**Always check the planner's iter sidecar for escalations.** Read `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` (this iter's plan-agent narrative). If the planner intentionally skipped provers — signaled by the `(no prover dispatch this iter ...)` marker in `PROGRESS.md`'s `## Current Objectives` section, by a "user escalation" / "hard gate fired" notice in the sidecar, or by the planner's `planValidate.status: ok_intentional_skip` meta — you MUST write a concise alert banner to `TO_USER.md` naming:
175	- What the impasse is (one sentence).
176	- What options the planner has surfaced (one bullet per option, if applicable).
177	- Where to find full context (the sidecar path).
178	
179	The planner does NOT write to `USER_HINTS.md` for escalations — `USER_HINTS.md` is user-to-planner only. The escalation banner is your responsibility; you are the user-facing channel.
180	
181	**Rules:**
182	- Be extremely concise (1-2 sentences per item, listed in markdown format).
183	- If nothing relevant for the user is detected, leave the file completely empty.
184	
185	## Step 7: Dispatch review subagents
186	
187	You may dispatch read-only review subagents BEFORE writing your consolidated summary. They are audits whose findings you incorporate into `summary.md` / `recommendations.md`. Each runs as its own fresh-context Claude process; spawning two or more in one assistant message runs them in parallel, subject to the global `max_parallel` cap.
188	
189	### Where the catalog comes from
190	
191	Your invocation prompt contains an auto-generated **Available subagents** section listing every enabled descriptor. Reviewers are typically marked `[read-only]`. A descriptor tagged `[MANDATORY]` for this phase MUST be dispatched before the phase completes. Do not `ls .archon/subagents/` — the catalog you were handed is authoritative.
192	
193	When you decide to invoke a specific reviewer, read its full prompt and directive shape from `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
194	
195	### How to dispatch
196	
197	Pattern (Bash tool, parallel-able, run **in foreground** so the report lands before you write your summary):
198	
199	```
200	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
201	  --name <subagent-name> \
202	  --slug <kebab-case-slug> \
203	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
204	  --write-domain 'task_results/**'
205	```
206	
207	Each directive must be **fully self-contained** — the reviewer reads only what the directive points it at. The directive shape for each reviewer is documented in its descriptor under `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
208	
209	### When NOT to dispatch
210	
211	- If this session was a pure proof-filling round with no new definitions or refactors, skip reviewers. They add latency and cost for no value.
212	- If a previous session in the last 3 iters already ran the same audit on the same scope, don't repeat unless that scope changed.
213	- The plan agent may also have dispatched reviewers proactively — check `task_results/` for matching reports before doubling up.
214	
215	### Incorporating findings
216	
217	After the reviewers return, read every report. Land each finding in your `summary.md` and `recommendations.md`:
218	
219	- **CRITICAL / HIGH** findings → bullet at the top of `recommendations.md` with the suggested action.
220	- **MEDIUM** findings → bullet in `recommendations.md`'s body.
221	- **LOW** findings → one-liner in `summary.md`'s notes section.
222	
223	Do NOT repeat the full report content in your summary — link to the report file. The plan agent reads your summary, not the raw reports.
224	
225	## Step 8: Self-Validation
226	
227	After writing all files, validate your output by checking:
228	- [ ] milestones.jsonl has valid JSON on every line
229	- [ ] Each milestone has `target.file`, `target.theorem`, `status`
230	- [ ] Each non-blocked milestone has at least 1 attempt with `code_tried` or `strategy`
231	- [ ] Number of attempts per milestone is proportional to edits in `attempts_raw.jsonl`
232	- [ ] summary.md includes specific code/errors, not just high-level summaries
233	- [ ] recommendations.md includes actionable next steps
234	- [ ] You did NOT add or remove any `\leanok` marker yourself (those are handled by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase that ran before you)
235	- [ ] For every Mathlib-backed declaration the prover reported, the blueprint chapter has `\mathlibok` (your domain)
236	- [ ] Any `\lean{...}` macro rename flagged in a `task_results/<file>.md` has been applied
237	- [ ] No `\notready` marker remains on a block whose Lean declaration now exists
238	
239	## Permissions
240	
241	You may write to:
242	- `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
243	- `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md`
244	- `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` — markers (`\leanok`, `\mathlibok`), `\lean{...}` macro corrections, stale-marker cleanup, and `% NOTE:` comments. Do NOT rewrite the informal prose — that is the plan agent's surface.

249	- `.archon/task_pending.md` or `.archon/task_done.md` (plan agent's responsibility)
250	- Blueprint informal content (theorem/proof prose, chapter structure) — only the markers and `% NOTE:` comments are yours.

1	# Mathlib Analogist Report
2	
3	## Slug
4	lieAlgebra-rank-bridge-iter129
5	
6	## Iteration
7	129
8	
9	## Question
10	
11	For the iter-128 body of `AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity`
12	(presheaf-evaluation-at-⊤ + extendScalars-to-k along the identity-section
13	ring map), does the iter-130+ rank lemma `cotangentSpaceAtIdentity_finrank_eq`
14	admit a tractable closure through Mathlib b80f227, or does the
15	presheaf-vs-sheaf coincidence cost flagged by `strategy-critic-iter129`
16	require a presentation change first?
17	
18	## Verdicts (summary)
19	
20	| Decision | Verdict | Severity |
21	|---|---|---|
22	| Iter-128 body's rank correctness (A in directive) | **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** | **critical / must-fix** |
23	| Standalone scheme-level cotangent sheaf alternative (B in directive) | **PROCEED** (do not pursue Replacement C this iter) | informational |
24	| Iter-130+ rank-lemma closure path (C in directive) | **PROCEED** with Replacement (B) — affine-chart base change | major |
25	
26	## Overall verdict
27	
28	**ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB — must replace the iter-128 body before stating the rank lemma.**
29	
30	The iter-128 body of `cotangentSpaceAtIdentity` computes the **zero `k`-module**
31	for every smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme `G/k` with
32	relative dimension `n ≥ 1` (i.e. for every consumer in the target class).
33	The rank lemma `finrank cotangentSpaceAtIdentity = n` is therefore
34	**provably FALSE as stated** against this body for `n ≥ 1`.
35	
36	The strategy-critic-iter129 was correct: the presheaf-side global
37	sections on a non-affine `G` differ from the sheaf-side, and the
38	divergence cannot be bridged without infrastructure that does not exist
39	in Mathlib b80f227.
40	
41	## Must-fix-this-iter
42	
43	**`AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/GrpObj.lean:102-116` — replace the body of
44	`cotangentSpaceAtIdentity`** before iter-130 dispatches a rank-lemma
45	prover lane. The current body is divergent-and-wrong (not merely
46	divergent-with-cost): it produces zero, not a rank-`n` module.
47	
48	Concrete fix path (**Replacement (B): affine-chart base change**):
49	
50	1. Extract via `smooth_locally_free_omega` an affine chart
51	   `V ⊆ G.left` containing the identity-section image `η(pt)`, with
52	   `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension n Γ(Spec k, U) Γ(G, V)`.
53	2. Build `ψ_V : Γ(G, V) ⟶ k` from the identity section restricted to V,
54	   composed with `Scheme.ΓSpecIso`.
55	3. Define the body as
56	   `(ModuleCat.extendScalars ψ_V.hom).obj (ModuleCat.of Γ(G, V) Ω[Γ(G, V) ⁄ Γ(Spec k, U)])`.
57	
58	This replaces the presheaf-at-⊤ evaluation with a chart-base-change
59	evaluation, dropping the canonical/sheaf-side claim but enabling the
60	rank lemma to close in 50-100 LOC against Mathlib's already-shipped
61	`Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank_kaehlerDifferential`
62	and the project's already-shipped `smooth_locally_free_omega`.
63	
64	**Tradeoff acknowledged**: Replacement (B) is non-canonical (depends
65	on chart choice via `Classical.choice`). Document with a `% NOTE` in
66	the docstring. For the rigidity-over-`k̄` consumer (the only live
67	consumer), the existence statement is all that matters; canonicity
68	would only be needed if a future Lie-algebra-bracket consumer
69	emerges, in which case Replacement (A) — stalk-side cotangent — would
70	need to be undertaken (estimated 500-1000 LOC).
71	
72	## Major
73	
74	- **Strategy LOC envelope under the corrected approach**: piece (i)
75	  stays at the bundled 800-1500 LOC estimate. The strategy-critic-iter129's
76	  upper-bound revision to 1300-2500 LOC (for Replacement C = sheafified Ω)
77	  is **NOT** triggered.
78	- **Iter cost for the rank lemma under Replacement (B)**: 1-2 prover iters.
79	
80	## Informational
81	
82	- **Replacement (A) (stalk-side `IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace`)** is the
83	  canonical mathematical object but requires bridging "standard smooth
84	  over a field at a prime ⇒ `IsRegularLocalRing` of dim n", which is
85	  a confirmed Mathlib gap (~300-600 LOC bridge cost). Out of scope
86	  this iter; document for future generality.
87	- **Replacement (C) (sheafified scheme-level `Ω`)** is the full
88	  Mathlib-aligned construction; defer indefinitely (~800-2000 LOC) unless
89	  a downstream non-rigidity consumer emerges. The iter-127 strategy's
90	  "iter-129 unbundling re-evaluation" trigger is **NOT** activated:
91	  Replacement (B) gives the same rank lemma at much lower cost.
92	
93	## Mathlib lemma chain (closure path under Replacement B)
94	
95	| Step | Lemma | Status |
96	|---|---|---|
97	| 1 | `AlgebraicGeometry.IsSmoothOfRelativeDimension.exists_isStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension` | [verified] |
98	| 2 | `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth` | [verified] |
99	| 3 | `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential` | [verified] |
100	| 4 | `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank_kaehlerDifferential` (`Module.rank Γ(V) Ω[Γ(V)/k] = n`) | [verified] |
101	| 5a | `Module.Free.tensorProduct` (base change preserves Free) | [expected — Mathlib has this pattern] |
102	| 5b | `Module.finrank_tensorProduct` / `Module.finrank_baseChange` | [expected — Mathlib has the pattern] |
103	| 6 | `Module.finrank_eq_rank'` (`finrank` ↔ `rank` under `FiniteDimensional`) | [verified] |
104	| 7 | `Scheme.ΓSpecIso` for the `ψ_V : Γ(G, V) → k` identification | [verified] |
105	| 8 | Auxiliary: `KaehlerDifferential.subsingleton_of_surjective` (used in Decision 1 to PROVE the iter-128 body is zero — diagnostic only) | [verified] |
106	| 9 | Auxiliary: `IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace` (used in Replacement A; not needed under B) | [verified] |
107	| 10 | Auxiliary: `AlgebraicGeometry.isField_of_universallyClosed` (proves `Γ(G, ⊤)` is a field for proper geo-integral G; diagnostic for Decision 1) | [verified] |
108	
109	**No Mathlib gaps under Replacement (B).** Every step is consumable
110	from Mathlib b80f227.
111	
112	## Why the iter-128 body collapses (diagnostic for the record)
113	
114	Step-by-step proof that the iter-128 body equals the zero `k`-module
115	for the target class:
116	
117	1. `(relativeDifferentialsPresheaf G.hom).obj (op ⊤) =
118	   CommRingCat.KaehlerDifferential (φ'.app (op ⊤))` by
119	   `relativeDifferentials'_obj` (rfl).
120	
121	2. `φ'.app (op ⊤)` has domain
122	   `((TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf).obj (op ⊤)`,
123	   computed as the left Kan extension colimit
124	   `colim_{V : Opens(Spec k), f.base(⊤_{G.left}) ⊆ V} Γ(Spec k, V)`.
125	   Since `Spec k` is single-point, the indexing cone is `{V = ⊤}`,
126	   so the colimit collapses to `Γ(Spec k, ⊤) ≅ k`.
127	
128	3. The codomain is `Γ(G.left, ⊤)`. Smooth ⇒ geometrically reduced;
129	   smooth proper geometrically irreducible ⇒ geometrically integral.
130	   For proper geometrically integral `G/k`, `Γ(G, ⊤) = k`
131	   (Stacks 0BUG; Hartshorne III.10.7; in Mathlib as
132	   `AlgebraicGeometry.isField_of_universallyClosed` + finite-extension
133	   + geometric-irreducibility argument).
134	
135	4. So `φ'.app (op ⊤) : k → k`, which is the structure map (i.e.
136	   identity up to canonical iso). By
137	   `KaehlerDifferential.subsingleton_of_surjective`,
138	   `CommRingCat.KaehlerDifferential (id_k) ≅ 0`.
139	
140	5. `(ModuleCat.extendScalars ψ.hom).obj (0_{Γ(G,⊤)-module}) = 0_{k-module}`.
141	
142	This is exactly the presheaf-vs-sheaf collapse that strategy-critic-iter129
143	warned about. The strategy-critic's concern was concrete and correct.
144	
145	## Persistent file
146	
147	- `analogies/lieAlgebra-rank-bridge.md` — full design-rationale captured
148	  for future iters. Documents:
149	  - Why the iter-128 body collapses (the 5-step diagnostic above).
150	  - The three replacement options (A: stalk-side, B: affine-chart, C:
151	    sheafified) with LOC estimates and Mathlib-coverage analysis.
152	  - The chosen replacement (B) and its closure-path bridge lemma list.
153	  - The presheaf-vs-sheaf coincidence theorem (absence) and why
154	    replacement (B) sidesteps it.
155	
156	## Reviewer's note for the plan agent
157	
158	Iter-130's rank-lemma prover lane should **NOT** be dispatched against
159	the current iter-128 body. The plan agent should first either:
160	
161	(a) **Direct a prover lane to refactor `AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/GrpObj.lean`**
162	to Replacement (B): swap the body to chart-base-change, keeping the
163	signature (which is already in `archon-protected.yaml` per the iter-129
164	rename, if applicable — verify). The rename to `cotangentSpaceAtIdentity`
165	is preserved; only the body changes.
166	
167	(b) **Or queue a combined refactor+rank lemma in a single iter-130 lane** —
168	the refactor is small (~50 LOC body swap) and the rank lemma is short
169	(~50-100 LOC); both fit in one prover iter.
170	
171	Either way, the iter-128 body **must** be replaced before the rank
172	claim can compile (let alone be true). Skipping this step would push
173	the project into a CHURNING regime as the iter-130 prover lane
174	discovers the body is zero and bounces.

47	## Alternative routes (suggested)
48	
49	### Alternative: Direct ℙ¹ rigidity for the `C(k) ≠ ∅` branch of M2.b
50	
51	- **What it looks like**: Split `genusZeroWitness` into two arms by Boolean classifier `Nonempty (𝟙_ _ ⟶ C)`. On the empty arm, `isAlbaneseFor` is vacuous — done. On the nonempty arm, use a *direct* rigidity for `f : ℙ¹_k → A` that bypasses the general "smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus 0 → group scheme" formulation. The ℙ¹-specific rigidity uses the explicit affine chart cover of ℙ¹: a morphism `Spec k[t] → A` factors through `Spec k` because the local cotangent at the closed point of A is trivialised by the smoothness of A, and the global `Spec k[t] → A` can be analyzed by looking at where `t` goes (a section of `O_A` near the image, which must be constant if `A` is proper). This avoids ever needing `lieAlgebra` of a general group scheme.
52	- **Why it might be cheaper or sounder**: ℙ¹ has an explicit Mathlib API (well, `AlgebraicGeometry.Proj` of `MvPolynomial.gradedAlgebra`); the affine chart cover gives a constructive entry point. The strategy's pile (i)+(ii)+(iii) totals 1850–3600 LOC for general curves; the ℙ¹-specific path would be in the 500–1000 LOC range because pieces (ii) and (iii) collapse to their ring-side `k[t]` / `k[1/t]` instances.

The strategy's framing — "the genus-0 universal property of J = Spec k routes through `rigidity_over_k`" — is over-general for what the universal property actually requires.

The iter-128 `lieAlgebra` definition uses `(ModuleCat.extendScalars ψ.hom).obj (M.obj (op ⊤))` where `M = relativeDifferentialsPresheaf G.hom`. For a proper non-affine `G`, the global sections of the presheaf at top differ from the sheaf-level global sections (sheafification matters). The rank lemma `finrank lieAlgebra = n` must implicitly assert that the chosen presheaf-globalsections-extendScalars construction is isomorphic to the geometric pullback `η^* Ω` along the identity section — and this isomorphism requires either (i) the shear-iso translation-invariance (which couples to piece (i.b), iter-130+, properly costed); or (ii) a separate "presheaf-vs-sheaf bridge at top" theorem the strategy has not costed. 103	## Overall verdict
104	
105	A fresh mathematician would approve **M1's excise** (sound, well-reasoned) and **M3's user-hint absorption + off-loop posture** (sound, deferring to long-build work). However **M2 carries multiple material concerns**: the iter-128-landed `lieAlgebra` Lean definition is dimensionally too narrow (hardcoded `n = 1`) for the rigidity argument's actual codomain `A`; the chosen construction re-imports a presheaf-vs-sheaf bridge that iter-126's M1 excise was supposed to remove; the over-k commitment's net savings collapsed to a lower bound of zero without re-defense; the standalone-cotangent-sheaf alternative's iter-129 re-evaluation trigger fires this iter but is not actively engaged; and the revert trigger (a) is mis-targeted to iter-128 piece (i.a) when it should target the iter-130+ piece (i.b) shear-iso lane. Five must-fix issues land on the planner this iter. The strategy is not REJECT-grade (the math is plausible and the routes are defensible if the issues are addressed), but it's solidly CHALLENGE — the planner must either update STRATEGY.md or record an explicit rebuttal in `iter/iter-129/plan.md` for each of the five.

46	  1. Bridge to $\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}/\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}^2$ via `lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge`, anchored on `Ideal.IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace`.
47	  2. Regularity of the local ring at the identity: smooth ⇒ `IsRegularLocalRing` (Stacks 00TR / Hartshorne II.10.3.B); Krull dim = relative dim $n$ (Stacks 02G1).
48	  3. Cotangent rank = Krull dim for regular local rings (definitional property of `IsRegularLocalRing`).
49	  4. Cross-check via the affine-chart route: `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank_kaehlerDifferential` + `\cref{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega}` (in `AlgebraicJacobian/Differentials.lean` as `smooth_locally_free_omega`). Notes that this route is alternative — the bridge-via-regular-local-ring route is preferred as more directly aligned with the iter-128 body.
50	- Closing dual-rank step: $\finrank_k\,\mathfrak g = \finrank_k\,\mathfrak g^\vee = n$ via rank-equals-rank-of-dual.
51	- "Mathlib name summary" paragraph (mirroring the `Differentials.tex` model) listing the 4 closure pieces: `Ideal.IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace`, `IsRegularLocalRing`, the Krull-dim-equals-cotangent-rank fact, `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank_kaehlerDifferential` + `thm:smooth_locally_free_omega`.

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Differentials
7	import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.ChangeOfRings
8	import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Group.Smooth
9	import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper
10	import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Geometrically.Irreducible
11	
12	/-!
13	# Cotangent space at the identity of a group scheme
14	
15	The cotangent space at the identity of a smooth proper geometrically
16	irreducible group scheme `G` over a base field `k`, defined as a
17	`k`-vector space, via the pullback of the relative cotangent presheaf
18	`Ω_{G/k}` along the identity section `η_G : 𝟙_ ⟶ G`.
19	
20	This is piece (i.a) of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile (see
21	`blueprint/src/chapters/RigidityKbar.tex` § "Piece (i): sub-lemma
22	decomposition for iter-128+ build" and STRATEGY.md § M2.body-pile).
23	
24	The companion rank lemma `cotangentSpaceAtIdentity_finrank_eq` (rank =
25	relative dimension `n` from `[SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]`) is
26	deferred to iter-129+ for blueprint-RHS-pinning work; not in this file.
27	
28	## Status (iter-129 fix-up: signature relaxed + renamed; iter-128 body preserved)
29	
30	`AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity` (renamed from the
31	iter-128 `lieAlgebra`) is fully constructed (no `sorry`) via the
32	pullback-along-section bridge described in the declaration's
33	docstring. The signature has been relaxed from a hardcoded relative
34	dimension `1` to a free `{n : ℕ} [SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]`
35	binder, so the same definition serves any group scheme (including the
36	abelian-variety consumer `rigidity_over_kbar` of arbitrary relative
37	dimension `g`). The iter-129+ rank lemma
38	`cotangentSpaceAtIdentity_finrank_eq` lives in a follow-up declaration
39	(not in this file).
40	
41	## References
42	
43	- Blueprint chapter: `blueprint/src/chapters/RigidityKbar.tex` § "Piece (i)".
44	- Project infrastructure consumed:
45	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf`
46	  (in `AlgebraicJacobian.Differentials`).
47	- Mathlib `GrpObj` API: `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Grp_`,
48	  `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Group.Smooth`.
49	-/
50	
51	set_option autoImplicit false
52	
53	universe u
54	
55	open CategoryTheory Limits MonoidalCategory CartesianMonoidalCategory MonObj
56	open TopologicalSpace Opposite
57	
58	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj
59	
60	variable {k : Type u} [Field k]
61	
62	/-- The **cotangent space at the identity** of a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible group scheme `G` over `k`, as a `k`-vector space (returned as
64	`ModuleCat k`).
65	
66	Mathematically: `η_G^* Ω_{G/k}`, the pullback of the relative cotangent
67	sheaf along the identity section. By smoothness of `G` at `η_G` this is
68	a finitely-generated free `k`-module of rank equal to the relative
69	dimension of `G.hom`; the iter-129+ companion rank lemma
70	`cotangentSpaceAtIdentity_finrank_eq` (in a follow-up declaration) pins
71	the rank to `n` from the `[SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]` instance.
72	
73	The Lie algebra `𝔤` of `G` (the tangent space at the identity) is the
74	`k`-linear dual of this module; downstream consumers that need `𝔤` may
75	take `Module.Dual k (cotangentSpaceAtIdentity G)`. We do not dualise here
76	because the rigidity argument is symmetric under dualisation (only the
77	finite-rank-free claim is consumed) and the un-dualised form is the
78	direct output of the pullback-along-section construction.
79	
80	The bracket / Lie-algebra structure is NOT packaged here — only the
81	underlying `k`-module is needed for the rigidity argument.
82	
83	**Construction (iter-128 prover lane; signature relaxed iter-129).**
84	The body uses the pullback-along-section bridge:
85	1. The identity section `η_G : 𝟙_ ⟶ G` of the `GrpObj` structure gives
86	   a scheme morphism `η_G.left : Spec k ⟶ G.left` (using the definitional
87	   identification `(𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k)))).left = Spec (.of k)`).
88	2. Sections on the top open give a commutative ring map
89	   `Γ(G.left, ⊤) ⟶ Γ(Spec k, ⊤)`; composing with the canonical iso
90	   `Γ(Spec k, ⊤) ≅ k` (`Scheme.ΓSpecIso`) yields
91	   `ψ : Γ(G.left, ⊤) ⟶ CommRingCat.of k`.
92	3. `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf G.hom` evaluated at the top open
93	   yields a `Γ(G.left, ⊤)`-module, the module of global relative
94	   differentials of `G/k`.
95	4. Extension of scalars along `ψ` to `k` (i.e. tensoring with `k`
96	   over `Γ(G.left, ⊤)`) gives the cotangent space at the identity
97	   section as a `k`-module.
98	
99	This compiles to a `ModuleCat k` with no `sorry`. The structural
100	properties (`Module.Free k`, `Module.Finite k`, rank `= n`) are
101	content for the iter-129+ rank lemma. -/
102	noncomputable def cotangentSpaceAtIdentity (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
103	    [CategoryTheory.GrpObj G] {n : ℕ} [SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom] [IsProper G.hom]
104	    [GeometricallyIrreducible G.hom] :
105	    ModuleCat k :=
106	  -- Identity section `η_G : 𝟙_ ⟶ G`; on schemes this is `Spec k ⟶ G.left`.
107	  let ηleft : Spec (.of k) ⟶ G.left := CategoryTheory.CommaMorphism.left η[G]
108	  -- Ring map `Γ(G.left, ⊤) ⟶ k` from sections of the identity section,
109	  -- composed with the `Γ(Spec k, ⊤) ≅ k` adjunction iso.
110	  let ψ : Γ(G.left, ⊤) ⟶ CommRingCat.of k :=
111	    ηleft.appTop ≫ (Scheme.ΓSpecIso (.of k)).hom
112	  -- Relative cotangent presheaf evaluated at the top open of `G`.
113	  let M := Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf G.hom
114	  -- Extend scalars from `Γ(G.left, ⊤)` to `k` along `ψ`; this is the
115	  -- global-sections form of `η_G^* Ω_{G/k}`.
116	  (ModuleCat.extendScalars ψ.hom).obj (M.obj (op ⊤))
117	
118	end AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj

name: lean-auditor
description: Whole-project read-only audit of all .lean code, with no strategy bias. Per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, bad Lean practices. Critical pushback on excuse-comments ("temporary wrong def", "will fix later") — treats them as red flags, not workflow.
write_domain: "task_results/**"
read_only: true
can_spawn: false
default_enabled: true
mandatory: [review]
dispatcher_notes: |
  - Dispatch me every review phase. I am mandatory.
  - Pass me as LITTLE strategic context as possible. Do NOT include
    STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, or "this is what we are trying to prove"
    framing in the directive. My value depends on auditing the Lean
    *without* bias toward what the strategy claims should be true.
  - Acceptable directive content: a list of files, optional focus areas
    (e.g. "pay extra attention to Algebra/"), and the absolute paths
    to read. That's it.
  - My output is a per-file checklist plus a flagged-issues block. Use
    it to seed `recommendations.md` and (when severe) trigger a
    refactor-subagent directive next iter.
  - I am SEPARATE from lean-vs-blueprint-checker. I audit the Lean as
    Lean; the blueprint-checker audits Lean against blueprint. Run
    both for full coverage.
---

# Lean Auditor

You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.

You are **mandatory in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you every iteration. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.

## Stance: no strategic bias

You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.

This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:

- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`

are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.

## Directive Format

```markdown
# Lean Auditor Directive

## Slug
<slug>

## Scope (files)
<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>

## Focus areas (optional)
<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>

## Known issues
<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
```

The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.

## What you do

1. Read your directive.
2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
   - Read the whole file.
   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.

You may use:
- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.

---
name: lean-vs-blueprint-checker
description: Per-file bidirectional verifier. Reads one .lean file and its blueprint chapter; reports (a) whether the Lean file follows the blueprint — fake/placeholder statements, signature mismatches with \lean{...} hints, proof divergence, "temporary" defs — AND (b) whether the blueprint chapter is detailed enough to have guided a faithful formalization of this file.
write_domain: "task_results/**"
read_only: true
can_spawn: false
default_enabled: true
mandatory: [review]
dispatcher_notes: |
  - I am mandatory in the review phase. Dispatch one checker per .lean
    file that received prover work this iteration — every file, no
    exceptions. Skipping any prover-touched file is a review-phase
    failure, not editorial discretion. Each dispatch is independent
    and parallel-able.
  - Also dispatch on any file the lean-auditor flagged as suspicious,
    even if it received no prover work this iter.
  - The directive must name exactly one Lean file and its blueprint
    chapter — no global context, no strategy snapshot. My value is
    the narrow file-vs-chapter view.
  - I report bidirectionally: Lean → blueprint AND blueprint → Lean.
    The blueprint can be the failure (too thin to guide formalization,
    missing the level of detail the Lean code clearly needed), not
    only the Lean. If I flag a chapter as inadequate, the plan agent
    should dispatch a blueprint-writer to address it.
  - Any must-fix-this-iter finding I report blocks downstream work on
    the affected file/chapter until addressed. Soft enforcement: the
    plan agent reads my report and follows the gate rules in plan.md.
---

# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker

You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.

## Your Job

Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:

### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?

For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:

- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.

### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?

The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:

- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.

Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.

## Directive Format

```markdown
# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker Directive

## Slug
<slug>

## Lean file
<path/to/file.lean>

## Blueprint chapter
blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex

## Known issues
<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
```

That's it. No strategy snapshot, no references, no full project context — just the two files and any pre-known issues to skip. Cross-file consistency is the lean-auditor's and blueprint-reviewer's job; you stay focused on this one pair.

## What you do

1	# Analogy: cotangent-space-at-identity rank bridge — presheaf-at-⊤ collapse and the iter-130+ rank-lemma closure
2	
3	## Slug
4	lieAlgebra-rank-bridge
5	
6	## Iteration
7	129
8	
9	## Question
10	
11	For the iter-128 body
12	```
13	cotangentSpaceAtIdentity G :=
14	  let ψ : Γ(G.left, ⊤) ⟶ CommRingCat.of k := ηleft.appTop ≫ (Scheme.ΓSpecIso (.of k)).hom
15	  let M := Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf G.hom
16	  (ModuleCat.extendScalars ψ.hom).obj (M.obj (op ⊤))
17	```
18	of a smooth-proper-geometrically-irreducible group scheme `G/k`, does the
19	iter-130+ rank lemma `finrank k (cotangentSpaceAtIdentity G) = n`
20	(with `n` from `[SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]`) admit a tractable
21	closure path through Mathlib b80f227, or does the presheaf-side
22	evaluation at the top open hide a presheaf-vs-sheaf coincidence cost
23	that the rank lemma must pay before it can close?
24	
25	## Project artifact(s)
26	
27	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/GrpObj.lean:102-116` — `cotangentSpaceAtIdentity G` body (iter-128 prover lane; iter-129 rename + signature relax).
28	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Differentials.lean:51-66` — `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf` definition + `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_obj_kaehler` `rfl` identification.
29	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Differentials.lean:124-143` — `smooth_locally_free_omega`, the load-bearing affine-chart Jacobian criterion that the rank lemma must consume.
30	
31	## Decisions identified
32	
33	### Decision 1: Does the iter-128 body of `cotangentSpaceAtIdentity` have rank `n` for the target class (smooth proper geometrically irreducible `G/k`, relative dim `n`)?
34	
35	- **Mathematical answer**: **NO**. The iter-128 body **vanishes** (zero `k`-module) for every `G` in the target class with `n ≥ 1`.
36	
37	  Step-by-step:
38	  1. The relative differentials *presheaf* `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf G.hom` evaluated at `op ⊤` is, by `relativeDifferentials'_obj` (rfl, `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Differentials.Presheaf`), `CommRingCat.KaehlerDifferential (φ'.app (op ⊤))` where
39	     `φ' : (TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf ⟶ G.left.presheaf`.
40	  2. `((TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf).obj (op ⊤)` is the pointwise left Kan extension colimit over `{V : Opens(Spec k) | f.base(⊤_{G.left}) ⊆ V}`. Since `Spec k` is a single-point space (opens = `{∅, ⊤}`) and `f.base(⊤_{G.left})` is the unique point, the indexing diagram is a singleton `{V = ⊤_{Spec k}}`. The colimit collapses to `Γ(Spec k, ⊤) ≅ k` (via `Scheme.ΓSpecIso`).
41	  3. For smooth proper geometrically irreducible `G/k`, smoothness implies geometrically reduced (smooth over a field ⇒ geometrically regular ⇒ reduced after base change), hence `G` is **geometrically integral**. By the Stacks-0BUG / Hartshorne III.10.7 result (in Mathlib as `AlgebraicGeometry.isField_of_universallyClosed`), `Γ(G, ⊤)` is a field finite over `k`; geometric irreducibility forces it to be `k` itself (dim 1 after base change to `k̄`).
42	  4. So `φ'.app (op ⊤)` is the structure map `k → Γ(G, ⊤) = k`, i.e. essentially the identity. By `KaehlerDifferential.subsingleton_of_surjective` (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic`), `Ω[k/k] = 0` (subsingleton).
43	  5. The iter-128 body is `k ⊗_{Γ(G,⊤)} Ω[Γ(G,⊤)/(pullback presheaf @ ⊤)] = k ⊗_k 0 = 0`.
44	
45	  The rank lemma `finrank cotangentSpaceAtIdentity = n` is therefore **provably FALSE for `n ≥ 1`** as stated against the iter-128 body. (For `n = 0`, the lemma holds vacuously but the construction is also useless.)
46	
47	- **Mathlib idiom (what would actually compute the rank)**: For a non-affine `G`, the correct algebraic-geometry object is `(η_G^* Ω_{G/k})(⊤)` where `Ω_{G/k}` is the **sheafified** relative differential **sheaf of modules** (not the presheaf). This sheaf has the property that its global sections on an affine chart `V ∋ η_G(pt)` coincide with the algebraic Kähler module, and pullback along the identity section then descends to a `k`-vector space of rank `n`. Mathlib b80f227 has the affine-chart side (`Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank_kaehlerDifferential` [verified, in `Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent`]) but **does not** ship the scheme-level sheafified `Ω` or the sheafified pullback-of-modules-along-section composite.
48	
49	- **Gap**: **divergent-and-wrong**. The iter-128 body is not merely fragile — it computes the *wrong mathematical object* (zero rather than rank-`n`) for the consumer class.
50	
51	- **Cost of divergence**: the rank lemma `cotangentSpaceAtIdentity_finrank_eq` cannot close against the iter-128 body. Any attempt by the iter-130 prover lane to ship this lemma will either (a) waste cycles trying to prove a false statement, or (b) accidentally close a vacuous version that disguises the bug behind setoid / canonical-iso obfuscation.
52	
53	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB**. The body must be replaced before the rank lemma can be stated, let alone proven.
54	
55	### Decision 2: Which replacement body should iter-129/130 land?
56	
57	Three candidates, with costs:
58	
59	**Replacement (A): Stalk-side cotangent — `IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace O_{G, η(pt)}`.**
60	
61	- Mathlib backing:
62	  - `IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace` (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Cotangent`) [verified].
63	  - `IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace` (`Mathlib.RingTheory.RegularLocalRing.Defs`) [verified]:
64	    `IsRegularLocalRing R ↔ Module.finrank (ResidueField R) (CotangentSpace R) = ringKrullDim R`.
65	  - `AlgebraicGeometry.LocallyRingedSpace.isLocalRing` [verified]: every stalk is a local ring.
66	- Required bridge: **smooth over a field at a point ⇒ regular local ring of dim n at that point**. Mathlib b80f227 ships `Algebra.smoothLocus_eq_univ` (smooth ⇒ everywhere smooth) and `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank_kaehlerDifferential` (rank of Ω = n) but **does NOT** ship a direct "standard-smooth-over-field at prime ⇒ `IsRegularLocalRing` of dim n" lemma. Status: **[gap]**. The bridge requires manually composing:
67	  1. Affine chart `V` with `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension n k Γ(V)` (from `smooth_locally_free_omega`).
68	  2. Localize at the prime corresponding to `η(pt)` to get a local ring `Γ(V)_p`.
69	  3. `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension` is preserved by localization (Mathlib has the analogue for `Algebra.Smooth` but the standard-smooth-of-dim-n variant needs care).
70	  4. Show `Γ(V)_p` is regular local of dim n: the Kähler module of `Γ(V)_p / k` is free of rank n (base change from the appLE algebra), tensor with the residue field gives the cotangent space, and chasing dimensions against Krull dim requires `Algebra.FormallySmooth.iff_injective_lTensor_residueField`-style reasoning [verified, in `Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.Local`].
71	  5. Identify the stalk `O_{G, η(pt)}` with `Γ(V)_p` via `AlgebraicGeometry.StructureSheaf.IsLocalization.to_stalk` [verified].
72	  6. Identify the residue field with `k` via the identity section.
73	- **Estimated LOC**: 500-1000. The localization-preserves-standard-smooth-of-dim-n step and the regular-local equivalence chase are non-trivial.
74	- **Canonicity**: yes — independent of chart choice.
75	
76	**Replacement (B): Affine-chart base change — `k ⊗_{Γ(V)} Ω[Γ(V)/k]` for a chosen affine `V ∋ η(pt)`.**
77	
78	- Mathlib backing:
79	  - `smooth_locally_free_omega` (already shipped in project) — gives free Ω of rank n on the chart.
80	  - `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank_kaehlerDifferential` [verified] — gives the rank claim.
81	  - `Module.finrank_tensorProduct` / `Module.Free.rank_tensorProduct` for base-change-preserves-finrank [verified family].
82	- **Estimated LOC**: 200-400. Most cost is sigma-typing the chart choice + bridging `Module.rank` ↔ `Module.finrank` claims through the existing `smooth_locally_free_omega` shape.
83	- **Canonicity**: NO — the body depends on a chosen chart. Mitigation: bundle the chart choice into a `Nonempty` existential ("there exists a chart...") and define the cotangent via `Classical.choice`. Downstream consumers (rigidity argument) only need the existence of a rank-`n` `k`-module, not canonicity.
84	
85	**Replacement (C): Sheafify Ω first — full Mathlib-aligned `Scheme.Omega` sheaf, then pull back along η.**
86	
87	- Mathlib backing: minimal. Mathlib has `TopCat.Sheaf.pullback` and `PresheafOfModules.sheafification` (limited to fixed-ring case) but not a scheme-level sheafified relative differential sheaf or a `PresheafOfModules.pullback` along a section for the ring-changing case.
88	- **Estimated LOC**: 800-2000 (matches the iter-127 strategy's bundled-piece (i) upper bound). This is the "iter-129 standalone-sheaf detour" scoped in `STRATEGY.md` § M2.body-pile.
89	- **Canonicity**: yes — fully canonical, matches blueprint mathematical content.
90	- **Cost-vs-benefit**: high LOC for a single rank lemma that doesn't need full sheaf machinery. Defer unless a downstream consumer requires sheaf operations on Ω (none currently identified in the live project graph).
91	
92	- **Verdict**: **Replacement (B) for iter-130, with a `% NOTE` documenting the bridge cost to (A)/(C) for future generality**.
93	
94	  Rationale:
95	  - The rigidity-over-`k̄` consumer only needs `∃ T : Type*, [Module k T] ∧ Module.finrank k T = g` for the abelian-variety genus. Canonicity of the cotangent definition is unnecessary.
96	  - Replacement (B) consumes the project's already-shipped `smooth_locally_free_omega` and Mathlib's already-shipped `rank_kaehlerDifferential` — no new Mathlib gaps.
97	  - Replacement (A) is the "correct" mathematical object but pays 500-1000 LOC for canonicity that the consumer does not exploit.
98	  - Replacement (C) is reserved for if/when a downstream non-rigidity consumer needs the cotangent **sheaf** (not vector space).
99	
100	### Decision 3: What is the iter-130+ rank-lemma closure path?

# User Hints

Write strategic guidance here. The plan agent reads this file every iteration.
Provers do not read this file — the plan agent translates your hints into concrete objectives.

## Critical iter-129 discovery (mathlib-analogist)

The single most consequential finding this iter is **out of band** of the
plan-phase HARD GATE / refactor / blueprint-writer apparatus: the iter-128
body of `cotangentSpaceAtIdentity` (formerly `lieAlgebra`) is
**mathematically degenerate** — it computes the zero `k`-module for every
smooth proper geometrically irreducible `G/k` with relative dimension
`n ≥ 1`, i.e. for **every consumer in the target class**. The 5-step
diagnostic:

1. `(relativeDifferentialsPresheaf G.hom).obj (op ⊤) = CommRingCat.KaehlerDifferential (φ'.app (op ⊤))` by `rfl`.
2. `((TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf).obj (op ⊤)` is a colimit over `{V : Opens(Spec k) | f.base(⊤) ⊆ V}`. Since `Spec k` is single-point, the diagram collapses to `Γ(Spec k, ⊤) ≅ k`.
3. Smooth + proper + geometrically irreducible ⇒ geometrically integral ⇒ `Γ(G, ⊤) = k` (Stacks 0BUG; in Mathlib as `AlgebraicGeometry.isField_of_universallyClosed`).
4. So `φ'.app (op ⊤) : k → k`, the structure map (essentially identity). By `KaehlerDifferential.subsingleton_of_surjective`, `Ω[k/k] = 0`.
5. `(ModuleCat.extendScalars ψ.hom).obj 0 = 0`.

The strategy-critic-iter129 had flagged this concern abstractly under its
M2 CHALLENGE "hidden presheaf-vs-sheaf bridge re-enters via the deferred
rank lemma"; the mathlib-analogist sharpened that abstract concern to a
**concrete vacuity claim** with a Mathlib-named diagnostic chain.

This is exactly the failure mode the iter-128 review-phase
lean-vs-blueprint-checker anticipated when it flagged the "bridge gap"
between the evaluate-then-extend-scalars Lean body and the
`𝔪/𝔪²` stalk presentation of the blueprint proof: there *is no*
canonical bridge — the presheaf-side global sections of a non-affine
scheme genuinely differ from the sheaf-side, and for proper geom-integral
`G` the difference is total annihilation of `Ω` rather than a finite
discrepancy.

**Status**: the iter-128 body is kernel-clean and `lean_verify`-clean,
but proves a vacuous claim (it is `ModuleCat k` of `0`, not the
mathematical cotangent space at the identity). The strategy-critic's
CHALLENGE was correct in substance and is now resolved by the iter-130

## Knowledge Base additions (this iter)

Three new Proof Patterns / soundness lessons folded into `PROJECT_STATUS.md § Knowledge Base`:

1. **Presheaf-pullback at top open of `Spec k` collapses to `k`** — `((TopCat.Presheaf.pullback CommRingCat f.base).obj S.presheaf).obj (op ⊤)` is the pointwise left Kan extension colimit over `{V : Opens(Spec k) | f.base(⊤) ⊆ V}`. Since `Spec k` is single-point, the indexing diagram is `{V = ⊤}` and the colimit collapses to `Γ(Spec k, ⊤) ≅ k`. **Consequence**: evaluating a presheaf-of-modules pullback along a `Spec k → X` morphism at the top open of `X` loses **all** information except the `k`-side structure map.
2. **Kernel-clean ≠ mathematically correct** — `lean_verify` returning kernel-only axioms means the *proof* uses only allowed axioms; it does NOT mean the *statement* is mathematically meaningful. The iter-128 body of `cotangentSpaceAtIdentity` is kernel-clean but produces the zero `k`-module for every consumer (when the math claims it should be rank-`n`). **Lesson**: a mathlib-analogist consult must be triggered when a new definition's construction differs structurally from the blueprint sketch (here: evaluate-first vs `𝔪/𝔪²` stalk), even if the Lean body type-checks.
3. **`Ideal.IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace` is the verified Mathlib name** — defined as `(maximalIdeal R).Cotangent` in `Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Cotangent`. The name `IsRegularLocalRing.cotangentSpace` (which the strategy + blueprint prose referenced earlier) does NOT exist; replace any future references.
